ZcguQt4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Helen Xu is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she conducts research in a group focused on systems and algorithms for large-scale data processing. Her work centers on the design and analysis of high-performance, write- and update-friendly data structures for graphs and ordered sets, with particular emphasis on **parallel algorithms**, **caching**, and **performance engineering**. She investigates practical and theoretical tradeoffs for <u>dynamic graph representations</u>, <u>packed memory arrays</u>, and <u>cache-adaptive/cache-oblivious</u> techniques to enable *efficient updates*, *low-overhead traversal*, and *high locality* in multicore and memory-constrained environments.

---

## Research Areas
Helen's research spans the intersection of data structures, parallelism, and memory systems. She has developed and evaluated new graph containers and compressed representations (e.g., *Packed Compressed Sparse Row*, *Batch-parallel CSR*) that balance update performance with locality for graph analytics. A recurring theme is the adaptation and optimization of the *Packed Memory Array (PMA)* paradigm — producing variants for parallelism, compression (CPMA), and search-optimized layouts (SPMA) — to support fast inserts, range scans, and cache-efficient traversals. Her work also addresses skewed, streaming graph workloads (e.g., *Terrace*), write-optimized ordered dictionaries (e.g., *write-optimized skip lists*, B-skiplist variants), and the interplay between caching policies and multicore concurrency (cache-adaptive analyses and multicore paging results). She combines theoretical analysis (competitive/cost models for caching and adaptivity) with extensive empirical evaluation and benchmarking (e.g., *BYO: a unified framework for benchmarking large-scale graph containers*), and explores applications ranging from SpGEMM optimizations and sparse tensor formats to novel uses of neuromorphic and RL-driven system optimizations.

---

## Notable Works
- "Packed compressed sparse row: A dynamic graph representation" (2018) — Introduced a compact, update-friendly variant of CSR that preserves the traversal and lookup performance of CSR while enabling efficient insertions and deletions, significantly influencing later dynamic graph containers.  
- "Terrace: A hierarchical graph container for skewed dynamic graphs" (2021) — Proposed a hierarchical container designed for streaming, degree-skewed graphs, enabling fast updates and analytics on real-world social and web graphs with heavy-tailed degree distributions.  
- "A parallel packed memory array to store dynamic graphs" (2021) — Adapted the Packed Memory Array for parallel, cache-efficient storage of dynamic graphs, demonstrating how PMA-style layouts can deliver both fast concurrent updates and locality for graph traversals.

---

## Academic Background
Helen Xu has built a substantial body of work at the confluence of algorithms, data structures, and systems, accumulating over **400 total citations** for contributions that span both theory and practice. Affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, she has collaborated with researchers across institutions (including earlier collaborations evident in projects involving Stony Brook and national laboratory coauthors) and published extensively on dynamic graph containers, PMA-based structures, caching theory, and performance engineering. Her publications appear in leading venues for parallel computing, data structures, and systems; she maintains a public research homepage at https://itshelenxu.github.io/ where code, papers, and benchmarks accompanying her work are documented. Her record demonstrates a consistent emphasis on creating portable, empirically validated data-structure designs that address real-world constraints of concurrency and memory hierarchy.

## Overview
Shamkant B. Navathe is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, a long‑standing leader in the field of **database systems**. He directs and contributes to research and educational efforts that bridge *fundamental theory*, *practical system design*, and *applied data management*. His work spans core topics such as <u>database design and modeling</u>, <u>schema integration</u>, <u>data fragmentation and distribution</u>, and <u>data mining for association and negative associations</u>. Navathe is also co‑author of the widely used textbook **Fundamentals of Database Systems**, a *foundational* resource in database education with broad international adoption. Over his career his scholarship has been highly influential (total citations ~34,500), reflecting both deep theoretical contributions and practical tools for database designers and practitioners.

---

## Research Areas
Navathe’s research agenda emphasizes principled methods for designing, integrating, partitioning, and querying database systems. In conceptual modeling and design he has advanced the practice of <u>entity‑relationship modeling</u> (see "Conceptual database design: an entity-relationship approach" (1991)) and developed interactive tools and methodologies for <u>integrating user views and schemas</u> ("Integrating user views in database design" (1986); "A tool for integrating conceptual schemas and user views" (1988)). In schema integration and automation he contributed formal foundations and reasoning approaches ("A theory of attributed equivalence in databases with application to schema integration" (2002); "On automatic reasoning for schema integration" (1993); "A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986)). Navathe has also produced influential work on physical design and fragmentation: a suite of papers on <u>vertical partitioning</u> and objective functions for fragmenting relations ("Vertical partitioning algorithms for database design" (1984); "Vertical partitioning for database design: a graphical algorithm" (1989); "An objective function for vertically partitioning relations in distributed databases and its analysis" (1994)). His interests extend into temporal extensions to the relational model ("A temporal relational model and a query language" (1989); "Temporal Extensions to the Relational Model and SQL." (1993)), automated index selection and query optimization ("Adaptive and automated index selection in RDBMS" (1992)), and data mining methods including algorithms for mining <u>association rules</u> and <u>negative associations</u> ("An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995); "Mining for strong negative associations in a large database of customer transactions" (1998)). Navathe’s work has also interfaced with applied domains — notably bioinformatics — through database construction efforts such as the MITOMAP mitochondrial genome database ("MITOMAP: a human mitochondrial genome database" (1996) and subsequent updates).

## Notable Works
- "Fundamentals of database systems" (2008) — A widely adopted textbook co‑authored by Navathe that synthesizes foundational concepts in data modeling, languages, and DBMS implementation; this work has been translated and issued in multiple editions and languages and stands as a cornerstone of database education.
- "An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995) — A highly cited algorithmic contribution that advanced scalable methods for discovering association rules in transaction data and influenced subsequent research in frequent pattern mining.
- "A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986) — A seminal study that systematically evaluated schema integration techniques, shaping research and practice in view integration, schema mapping, and the theoretical limits of automated integration.

## Academic Background
Navathe’s academic career spans multiple decades of scholarship, teaching, and mentorship at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he has established a research profile at the intersection of database theory and system design. His publication record begins in the late 1970s and continues through recent work, reflecting sustained contributions to core database problems and their applications. He is the co‑author of multiple editions of a leading database textbook and of influential research articles on schema integration, partitioning, temporal databases, and data mining; several of these works have been translated into other languages, indicating broad international impact. Navathe has collaborated widely across academia and applied domains (including bioinformatics projects such as MITOMAP), supervised graduate students, and contributed to curriculum development in database systems. His body of work — cumulative citations approximately 34,500 — attests to his role as a *foundational* and *enduring* figure in database research and education.
